questions.stem: REST interview opener: what does a resource-oriented design emphasize?
options: Nouns as resources identified by URIs; manipulate them with uniform HTTP methods
RPC procedure names encoded only in JSON-RPC envelopes
SOAP envelopes required for every public internet API
Verbs as path segments with no use of HTTP methods
questions.explanation: REST centers on resources and a uniform interface over HTTP.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Safe HTTP methods — which set is correct? Which answer is best?
options: POST and PUT are always safe by HTTP definition
GET, HEAD, and OPTIONS should not alter server state as a side effect
DELETE is safe because it removes ambiguity
PATCH is safe and idempotent in all RFCs without exception
questions.explanation: Safe methods are read-oriented and shouldn't change resources.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Idempotent methods among the common verbs? Which answer is best?
options: Only POST is idempotent by specification
PATCH is guaranteed idempotent in every API
GET, PUT, DELETE (and usually HEAD/OPTIONS); POST typically is not
No HTTP method is considered idempotent
questions.explanation: Idempotent ⇒ repeating the request yields the same server effect.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: When is POST the right choice over PUT? Which answer is best?
options: Replacing a known resource at a client-specified URI idempotently
Fetching a representation without side effects
Deleting a resource that must vanish on every retry
Creating a resource when the server assigns the URI/ID, or non-idempotent actions
questions.explanation: POST is for create-unknown-URI and non-idempotent processing.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: PUT semantics for a resource URI? Which answer is best?
options: Replace (or create) the target resource with the request representation
Always partially update a few fields like PATCH
Never create resources; only update existing ones
Ignore the body and only update timestamps
questions.explanation: PUT is full replacement at a known URI; repeats stay idempotent.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: PATCH versus PUT — crisp distinction? Which answer is best?
options: PATCH must always be idempotent; PUT must never be
PATCH applies a partial modification; PUT replaces the whole representation
PUT cannot create resources; PATCH always creates collections
They are identical methods with legacy alias names
questions.explanation: PATCH is partial update; PUT swaps the entire document.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: HTTP 201 Created — when should you return it? Which answer is best?
options: For every successful GET of an existing document
When validation fails on required fields
After successfully creating a resource, often with Location of the new URI
Only when a DELETE removes the last item
questions.explanation: 201 signals creation; Location commonly points at the new resource.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: HTTP 204 No Content typical use? Which answer is best?
options: Client error when the Accept header is missing
Redirect to a canonical HTTPS URL
Server overload asking clients to retry later
Success with intentionally empty body, e.g. after DELETE or some PUTs
questions.explanation: 204 means OK but nothing to return in the body.

questions.stem: In an interview setting, consider this question: 401 versus 403 — interview distinction?? Pick the strongest answer.
options: 401: unauthenticated/missing or bad credentials; 403: authenticated but not allowed
403 means login form required; 401 means permanently banned
They are interchangeable status codes in RFC 9110
401 is only for API keys; 403 only for cookies
questions.explanation: 401 challenges auth; 403 denies an identified principal.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: 404 vs 410 for missing resources? Which answer is best?
options: 410 means try again; 404 means delete your client cache
404: not found (maybe temporary); 410: gone permanently and won't return
Both require a WWW-Authenticate header always
404 is only for collections; 410 only for items
questions.explanation: 410 is a stronger permanent removal signal than 404.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: 409 Conflict — classic REST scenario? Which answer is best?
options: The request used an unsupported media type exclusively
The client must authenticate before any further calls
Update collides with current state (version conflict) or business rule clash
The server is down for scheduled maintenance
questions.explanation: 409 signals the request conflicts with the resource's current state.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: 429 Too Many Requests — meaning? Which answer is best?
options: The JSON body failed schema validation
The resource moved to a new permanent URI
The gateway could not find an upstream
Client hit a rate limit; often includes Retry-After guidance
questions.explanation: 429 tells clients to slow down; respect Retry-After when present.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Accept header role in content negotiation? Which answer is best?
options: Client declares preferred response media types; server may choose or 406
Server tells the client which body format it will ignore
It replaces Authorization for bearer tokens
It forces the server to always return application/xml
questions.explanation: Accept drives which representation the client can handle.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Content-Type on requests means? Which answer is best?
options: Lists every status code the client understands
Describes the media type of the request body the client is sending
Selects which database shard should handle the write
Disables caching for intermediary proxies always
questions.explanation: Content-Type labels the entity body format on the wire.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: HATEOAS — what would you say it adds? Which answer is best?
options: All APIs must abandon JSON for HTML forms only
Clients hard-code every URI template forever
Responses include hypermedia links guiding available next actions
Servers may never change link relations over time
questions.explanation: Hypermedia affords discoverable workflows beyond out-of-band docs.

questions.stem: In an interview setting, consider this question: URI design smell in REST interviews?? Pick the strongest answer.
options: Using plural nouns for collections such as /orders
Nesting lightly like /users/42/orders for ownership
Stable resource identifiers that outlive representation changes
Encoding actions as verbs in paths like /users/123/activateDeleteAll
questions.explanation: Prefer resources + methods over RPC-ish verb paths.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Collection filtering via query string — good practice? Which answer is best?
options: Use query params for filters/sort/pagination while keeping collection URI stable
Create a new path segment for every filter combination permanently
Put filters only in custom headers that caches ignore
Require POST with empty path for all list operations
questions.explanation: Query strings keep the resource identity clear while varying views.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: ETag / If-None-Match enable what? Which answer is best?
options: Mandatory encryption of all JSON payloads
Conditional GET caching and optimistic concurrency on updates
Automatic API versioning via header alone
Disabling intermediary caches by specification
questions.explanation: Validators let clients and caches avoid transferring unchanged bodies.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Cache-Control: private vs public? Which answer is best?
options: Public forbids all caching; private forces CDN storage
They only apply to WebSocket upgrade responses
Private: browser-only; public: shared caches/CDNs may store the response
Private means the payload is encrypted at rest
questions.explanation: Visibility directives control whether shared caches may reuse responses.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Why prefer /v1 in URI or header for API versioning debates? Which answer is best?
options: Versioning is unnecessary if you never add fields
Only SOAP requires versions; REST forbids them
Changing field types never counts as a breaking change
Explicit versions manage breaking changes; headers vs paths are trade-offs
questions.explanation: Version strategy matters; communicate breaking changes deliberately.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: REST vs RPC — fair contrast? Which answer is best?
options: REST manipulates resources with uniform methods; RPC invokes named procedures
RPC cannot use HTTP as a transport ever
REST requires XML and RPC requires JSON exclusively
They always produce identical client coupling
questions.explanation: Different styles: resource state transfer vs procedure calls.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Idempotency-Key header — why do payment APIs use it? Which answer is best?
options: It encrypts card numbers before they leave the browser
Clients supply a key so retries of POST create/charge only once
It replaces TLS for PCI-compliant transports
Servers ignore it unless the method is GET
questions.explanation: Idempotency keys make unsafe methods safely retryable.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Cursor/keyset pagination response shape often includes? Which answer is best?
options: Only a total count with no way to fetch page two
Negative offsets to walk backward through SQL
A next_cursor or next link opaque to clients for the following page
A full table dump base64-encoded in one field
questions.explanation: Opaque cursors avoid leaking storage details and scale better.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Link header or _links in body — purpose? Which answer is best?
options: Replace status codes with English prose only
Force clients to use SOAP envelopes next
Disable CORS preflight for credentialed calls
Advertise related resources and pagination without out-of-band knowledge
questions.explanation: Links are the hypermedia affordance for navigation.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: What should a good error body include? Which answer is best?
options: Machine-readable code, human message, and optional field-level details
Only a stack trace with production secrets
An empty body for every 4xx and 5xx
HTML login pages for API clients always
questions.explanation: Structured errors help clients branch and display useful feedback.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Problem Details (RFC 7807) media type? Which answer is best?
options: text/csv for tabular validation failures only
application/problem+json for standardized HTTP API error payloads
multipart/form-data for every error response
application/octet-stream carrying Java stack dumps
questions.explanation: problem+json is the common standard for API error documents.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: CORS preflight (OPTIONS) happens when? Which answer is best?
options: Every same-origin GET triggers a mandatory preflight
Preflight replaces the need for Access-Control-Allow-Origin
Browser sends OPTIONS for non-simple cross-origin requests before the real call
Mobile native apps always issue CORS preflights
questions.explanation: Preflight checks whether the actual cross-origin request is allowed.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Same-origin policy impacts REST clients how? Which answer is best?
options: curl and server-side clients are blocked by SOP identically
SOP encrypts cookies automatically for all sites
It forbids JSON as a response media type
Browser JS can only call other origins if CORS headers permit it
questions.explanation: SOP is a browser rule; CORS is the opt-in escape hatch.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Bearer token in Authorization header form? Which answer is best?
options: Authorization: Bearer <token> for OAuth/JWT style API auth
Authorization: Basic with the raw JWT as password only
A custom Cookie forced without HttpOnly for SPAs
Query string ?access_token= for all confidential clients
questions.explanation: Bearer scheme is the standard header form for access tokens.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Why avoid tokens in query strings? Which answer is best?
options: Query strings cannot be HTTPS encrypted ever
They leak via logs, Referer headers, and browser history
Servers cannot read query parameters in HTTP/1.1
OAuth specs forbid refresh tokens in POST bodies
questions.explanation: Secrets in URLs are sticky in many incidental channels.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: POST for complex search — when is it defended? Which answer is best?
options: GET is forbidden for any read that returns JSON
POST is always cached better than GET by CDNs
Huge/complex filters exceed URL limits or shouldn't be logged in query strings
Search must never be safe or idempotent conceptually
questions.explanation: Pragmatic POST-search exists; document caching/semantics carefully.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: 307 vs 302 redirect nuance often cited? Which answer is best?
options: 302 always preserves POST bodies per modern clients
307 means permanently moved like 301
Neither redirect type may include a Location header
307 preserves method/body; older 302 clients may rewrite to GET
questions.explanation: 307 is the method-preserving temporary redirect.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: 301 vs 308 for permanent redirects? Which answer is best?
options: 301 may change method historically; 308 keeps method/body on permanent move
308 is temporary; 301 is method-preserving always
Both forbid Location headers by specification
301 only applies to WebSocket endpoints
questions.explanation: 308 is permanent + method-preserving; prefer it when that matters.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: What does OPTIONS commonly advertise? Which answer is best?
options: The full database schema for the collection
Allowed methods and CORS-related headers for a target resource
OAuth refresh tokens in the response body
A mandatory JSON Schema for every query param
questions.explanation: OPTIONS is discovery for methods and CORS policy.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Bulk operations in REST — common patterns? Which answer is best?
options: One TCP connection per field update exclusively
Only WebSockets can express multi-resource changes
Batch endpoints, multipart, or async job resources returning 202
PUT to / with no body updates every row
questions.explanation: Batches and jobs keep HTTP semantics workable for bulk work.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: 202 Accepted signals what? Which answer is best?
options: Resource created and immediately available at Location
Validation failed on the JSON body
Client must switch protocols like WebSocket
Request accepted for async processing; completion happens later
questions.explanation: 202 acknowledges async work without implying final success.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Representation vs resource — precise wording? Which answer is best?
options: A resource is the conceptual entity; representations are formats returned/sent
They are exact synonyms in Fielding's dissertation
Representations exist only for XML, not JSON
Resources cannot have multiple representations
questions.explanation: One resource may offer JSON, XML, etc., as representations.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Level 3 Richardson Maturity — what's added? Which answer is best?
options: Only POX over HTTP without proper status codes
Hypermedia controls (HATEOAS) beyond resources and HTTP verbs
Single URI tunnel for all operations like XML-RPC
Abandoning media types entirely
questions.explanation: RMM level 3 is hypermedia-driven affordances.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Optimistic concurrency with If-Match? Which answer is best?
options: Server ignores validators and last-writes-wins always
If-Match is only valid on GET responses
Client sends ETag; server applies update only if current tag matches
ETags cannot be used for write protection
questions.explanation: If-Match implements conditional updates from validators.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Why map domain errors to proper 4xx/5xx instead of 200+error flag? Which answer is best?
options: HTTP forbids bodies on any non-200 response
Browsers cannot display JSON unless status is 200
Load balancers only retry on HTTP 200
Status codes drive caches, clients, and intermediaries; fake-200 breaks tooling
questions.explanation: Honoring status codes keeps the uniform interface useful.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: REST API security checklist item you'd prioritize? Which answer is best?
options: HTTPS everywhere, authn/z, input validation, and least-privilege data access
Disable TLS to simplify certificate rotation
Trust all Origin headers without allowlists
Return stack traces with SQL in production errors
questions.explanation: Transport security plus authz and validation are table stakes.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Rate-limit headers often include? Which answer is best?
options: Only Set-Cookie values for quota tracking
X-RateLimit-Limit/Remaining/Reset or RateLimit-* style fields
WWW-Authenticate for every successful 200
Content-Range for non-partial JSON lists
questions.explanation: Clients need visibility into quota consumption and reset time.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Webhooks as REST-ish callbacks — design tip? Which answer is best?
options: Require the provider to poll your DB credentials
Accept unsigned payloads from any IP blindly
Sign payloads, verify quickly, and respond 2xx then process async if heavy
Block all retries so missed events stay lost
questions.explanation: Verify authenticity and ack fast; do heavy work out of band.

questions.stem: GraphQL vs REST — balanced interview line?
options: REST cannot version; GraphQL cannot authenticate
GraphQL replaces HTTP status codes entirely
They are identical wire protocols with different logos
GraphQL flexible queries/one endpoint; REST cacheable resource URIs and uniform methods
questions.explanation: Different trade-offs around flexibility, caching, and tooling.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: gRPC compared to JSON REST — key differences? Which answer is best?
options: HTTP/2 + protobuf, stricter contracts, streaming; less human-debuggable than JSON
gRPC forbids TLS and only runs on HTTP/1.0
JSON REST cannot express errors or deadlines
Protobuf messages cannot version fields safely
questions.explanation: gRPC optimizes service-to-service contracts; REST/JSON favors ubiquity.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: What makes a REST API cacheable effectively? Which answer is best?
options: POST everything so intermediaries never store responses
GET with explicit Cache-Control/ETag/Vary and stable URIs for representations
Random query strings on every identical read
Omit validators so clients re-download always
questions.explanation: Cacheability is a REST constraint leveraged via HTTP caching.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Partial failure in multi-resource POST batch — options? Which answer is best?
options: Always return 200 with no indication which items failed
Fail silently and leave clients to re-GET everything
Document atomic vs best-effort; use 207 Multi-Status or per-item results
Use 101 Switching Protocols for each failed item
questions.explanation: Be explicit about atomicity and per-item outcomes.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Why prefer link relations over hard-coded path building? Which answer is best?
options: Hard-coded paths never break across API versions
Link relations disable content negotiation completely
Servers must not advertise URIs in responses
Clients follow server-provided links, surviving URI redesigns better
questions.explanation: Hypermedia reduces brittle client assumptions about URI structure.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: TRACE method — why often disabled? Which answer is best?
options: Can be abused in XST attacks and rarely needed for public APIs
It is required for all CORS preflight success
RFC mandates TRACE on every production origin
TRACE encrypts request bodies end-to-end
questions.explanation: TRACE echoes requests and is a security footgun — usually off.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: OpenAPI/Swagger value for REST teams? Which answer is best?
options: A replacement for HTTP that avoids status codes
Machine-readable contract for docs, codegen, mocks, and validation
A database migration tool for sharded Postgres
An authentication protocol superseding OAuth
questions.explanation: OpenAPI is the de facto interface description for HTTP APIs.
